elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we consider nonparametric clustering of $M$ independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) data streams generated from {\em unknown distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-002: The distributions of the $M$ data streams belong to $K$ underlying distribution clusters.
elements.sent-2411-003: Existing results on exponentially consistent nonparametric clustering algorithms, like single linkage-based (SLINK) clustering and $k$-medoids distribution clustering, assume that the maximum intra-cluster distance ($d_L$) is smaller than the minimum inter-cluster distance ($d_H$).
elements.sent-2411-004: First, in the fixed sample size (FSS) setting, we show that exponential consistency can be achieved for SLINK clustering under a less strict assumption, $d_I < d_H$, where $d_I$ is the maximum distance between any two sub-clusters of a cluster that partition the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our results show that SLINK is exponentially consistent for a larger class of problems than $k$-medoids distribution clustering.
elements.sent-2411-007: We also identify examples where $k$-medoids clustering is unable to find the true clusters, but SLINK is exponentially consistent.
elements.sent-2411-008: Then, we propose a sequential clustering algorithm, named SLINK-SEQ, based on SLINK and prove that it is also exponentially consistent.
elements.sent-2411-009: Simulation results show that the SLINK-SEQ algorithm requires fewer expected number of samples than the FSS SLINK algorithm for the same probability of error.
elements.sent-2411-010: We consider the problem of clustering independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) data streams generated from {\em unknown probability distributions under the nonparametric setting.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Algorithms to solve the general problem of clustering a set of data points \{$ X^{(1) , X^{(2) ,....., X^{(M)}$\}, with each data point $ X^{(i)}$ $\in \mathbb{R ^n $ have been well studied, see for example \cite{JMLR:v6:banerjee05b,KauRou87,gower1969minimum,johnson1967hierarchical,shalev-shwartz_ben-david_2014,huang2018hierarchical .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: These algorithms could be classified into two categories: partitional (e.g. $k$-means \cite{JMLR:v6:banerjee05b , $k$-medoids \cite{KauRou87 ) and hierarchical \cite{huang2018hierarchical (e.g. single-linkage (SLINK) \cite{gower1969minimum , complete-linkage (CLINK) \cite{johnson1967hierarchical ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The focus of these works was on the computational complexity of the clustering algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-014: For the setting where the data points to be clustered are i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-015: For this setting, distance metrics between distributions, e.g. maximum mean discrepancy (MMD), and Kolmogorov-Smirnov distance (KSD), are more suitable than Euclidean distance between the $X^{(i)}$'s \cite{JMLR:v13:gretton12a,sriperumbudur2012empirical,8651294 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The exponential consistency of linkage-based hierarchical clustering algorithms was studied in \cite{wang2020exponentially .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The performance of anomaly detection, a special case of clustering, was analyzed in the nonparametric setting in \cite{8651359,doi:10.1080/07474946.2017.1360086,sreenivasan2021sequential .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In \cite{8651294 , exponential consistency of $k$-medoids clustering was proved in the fixed sample-size (FSS) setting under the assumption that the maximum intra-cluster distance ($d_L$) is smaller than the minimum inter-cluster distance ($d_H$).}
elements.sent-2411-019: A similar result was later proved for the $k$-medoids clustering algorithm in the sequential setting in \cite{sreenivasan2023nonparametric .}
elements.sent-2411-020: In \cite{wang2020exponentially , linkage-based hierarchical clustering algorithms (including the SLINK algorithm) were also shown to be exponentially consistent in the FSS setting under the same assumption $d_L < d_H$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, settings where $d_L > d_H$ are also important.
elements.sent-2411-022: For example, we could have clusters that have large diameter, but are separated by distances that are smaller than the diameter: see \cite{NEURIPS2019_9b16759a , where a clustering problem with two such clusters is considered.}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we first show that single-linkage clustering (SLINK) in the FSS setting is exponentially consistent under a less strict assumption than $d_L < d_H$ for the underlying distribution clusters.
elements.sent-2411-024: We define $d_I$ to be the maximum distance between any two sub-clusters of a cluster that partition the cluster and require the assumption that $d_I < d_H$.
elements.sent-2411-025: Note that $d_I < d_L$ when there are more than two distinct points in a cluster.
elements.sent-2411-026: We also show numerical examples where SLINK is exponentially consistent while $k$-medoids clustering is not.
elements.sent-2411-027: Thus, we identify that different clustering algorithms require different conditions on the underlying distribution clusters.
elements.sent-2411-028: Then, we develop a nonparametric sequential clustering algorithm, SLINK-SEQ, based on SLINK.
elements.sent-2411-029: We show that SLINK-SEQ is also exponentially consistent.
elements.sent-2411-030: Furthermore, simulation results show that the proposed sequential SLINK clustering outperforms FSS SLINK clustering in terms of expected number of samples required for a given probability of error.
elements.sent-2411-031: The rest of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-032: In Section \ref{sec:model , we provide the problem statement and the required notation.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In Section \ref{sec:expSLINK , we prove the exponential consistency of SLINK clustering under the assumption $d_I < d_H$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Then, we propose the nonparametric sequential SLINK clustering algorithm in Section \ref{sec:seqSLINK and prove that it is exponentially consistent.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Simulation results and conclusions are presented in Sections \ref{sec:simulations and \ref{sec:conclusions , respectively.}}

elements.section-2411-003: Exponential consistency of SLINK in the fixed sample size setting
elements.sent-2411-065: In this section, we prove the exponential consistency of single linkage-based (SLINK) clustering in the FSS setting under the assumption that $d_I < d_H$.
elements.sent-2411-066: As mentioned earlier, this assumption is less strict than the assumption of $d_L < d_H$ used in the analysis of SLINK in \cite{wang2020exponentially,wangthesis .}
elements.sent-2411-067: SLINK can be summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-068: Initially, each data stream is considered to be a cluster, i.e., we start with $M$ clusters.
elements.sent-2411-069: Then, we find the two closest clusters, where the inter-cluster distance is the minimum distance between any sequence in one cluster from any sequence in the other cluster.
elements.sent-2411-071: The algorithm stops when the number of clusters is $K$ (assuming $K$ is known).
elements.sent-2411-072: If $d_H$ is known instead of $K$, the algorithm continues as long as the two closest clusters are closer than $d_H$.
elements.sent-2411-073: If the exact pairwise distances between the different data streams are provided as input to the SLINK algorithm, it can be argued in the following manner that the clustering output will be correct (under the assumption $d_I < d_H$).
elements.sent-2411-074: From the definition of $d_I$, we know that any sub-cluster of cluster $k$ is within $d_I$ of at least one other sequence in the same cluster $k$.
elements.sent-2411-075: From the definition of $d_H$, we know that for any sub-cluster of cluster $k$ the nearest neighbour from any other cluster is farther than $d_H$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Therefore, due to the assumption that $d_I < d_H$, during each step of SLINK sub-clusters belonging to the same true cluster will be chosen for merging till all $K$ clusters have been identified, i.e., there will be no error if the true pairwise distances are known.
elements.sent-2411-077: However, since the true distributions are assumed to be unknown, the SLINK algorithm is provided with the estimates of the pairwise MMD or KSD distances between the data streams.
elements.sent-2411-078: Now, we show that SLINK is exponentially consistent if $d_I < d_H$.
elements.sent-2411-079: We show the proof for the algorithm with MMD estimates.
elements.sent-2411-080: A similar proof can be provided even for the case with KSD based on the concentration results for KSD in \cite{8651294 .}
elements.sent-2411-082: The probability of error of SLINK is upper bounded as: $P_e \le a_f e^{-b_f n}$ for some $a_f, b_f > 0$ for sufficiently large $n$.

elements.sent-2411-176: We consider three examples to illustrate the difference between the distance assumptions between the clusters.

elements.sent-2411-286: In this section, we prove the exponential consistency of single linkage-based (SLINK) clustering in the FSS setting, also referred to as SLINK FSS, under the assumption that $d_I < d_H$.
elements.sent-2411-287: In the FSS setting, we have $n$ samples of each data sequence.
elements.sent-2411-288: First, the pairwise distance between each pair of data sequences is estimated (using MMD or KSD estimates).
elements.sent-2411-289: Then, the clustering of data sequences is carried out as follows.
elements.sent-2411-290: Initially, each data sequence is considered to be a cluster, i.e., we start with $M$ clusters.
elements.sent-2411-291: Then, we find the two closest clusters, where the inter-cluster distance is the minimum distance between any data sequence in one cluster from any data sequence in the other cluster.
elements.sent-2411-292: When the number of clusters $K$ is unknown, the algorithm continues as long as the two closest clusters are closer than $d_H$ and stops if the distance between the two closest clusters is greater than or equal to $d_H$.\footnote{Either the knowledge of the number of clusters, or the knowledge of the expected minimum distance between two clusters is required.}
elements.sent-2411-293: A lower bound on $d_H$ would also suffice.
elements.sent-2411-294: If the exact pairwise distances between the distributions of the different data sequences are provided as input to the SLINK algorithm, it can be argued in the following manner that the clustering output will be correct (under the assumption $d_I < d_H$).
elements.sent-2411-295: In practice, since the true distributions are assumed to be unknown, the SLINK algorithm is provided with the estimates of the pairwise MMD or KSD distances between the data sequences.
elements.sent-2411-296: In the following section, we analyze the performance of SLINK with estimated distances and prove exponential consistency.

elements.sent-2411-323: We now discuss the computational complexity of the SLINK-SEQ algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-324: At each time $n$, we do the following computations.
elements.sent-2411-325: Update the pairwise distances between the data sequences.
elements.sent-2411-326: There are $M(M-1)/2$ distances to be updated.
elements.sent-2411-327: The MMD can be updated using the sequential update in $O(n)$ computations.
elements.sent-2411-328: Therefore, the overall complexity of this step is of the order $O(nM^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-329: Updating the clusters using SLINK: SLINK starts from $M$ clusters and reduces the number of clusters one at a time to $K$ clusters.
elements.sent-2411-331: In each step of SLINK, the distance of the merged cluster with the other clusters needs to be updated.
elements.sent-2411-332: This would require $O(M-i)$ computations at step $i$.
elements.sent-2411-333: The overall complexity of this step is $O(M^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-334: Overall, the complexity grows at most quadratically in the number of data sequences $M$.
elements.sent-2411-335: Further simplification could be studied in future work.
elements.sent-2411-336: In the simulations, we run the clustering algorithms for multiple realizations of the data sequences and compare the final clustering output to the true clusters.
elements.sent-2411-337: An error is declared if the clustering output does not exactly match the true clusrers.
elements.sent-2411-338: We estimate $P_e$ by taking the ratio of the number of errors to the number of realizations simulated.
elements.sent-2411-339: In the case of sequential algorithms, we also determine the number of samples at which the algorithm makes a decision on the clusters for each realization.
elements.sent-2411-340: First, we consider three examples to illustrate the difference between the distance assumptions between the clusters.
elements.sent-2411-341: The three examples are: (1) 2 clusters, means of the sequences in cluster 1 and cluster 2 are $\{0.4, 0.55, 0.7, 0.85, 1, 1.15, 1.3, 1.45, 1.6\}$ and $\{1.85, 2, 2.15\}$, (2) 2 clusters, means of the sequences in cluster 1 and cluster 2 are $\{0.7, 0.85, 1, 1.15, 1.3\}$ and $\{1.7, 1.85, 2, 2.15, 2.3\}$, and (3) 5 clusters, 5 sequences per cluster, mean of each sequence in cluster $k$ is $k-1$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.
